coredata.dc:title: Laser welding defects detection in lithium-ion battery poles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Engineering Science and Technology, an International Journal
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Karabuk University
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Karabuk University
coredata.dc:creator: Din, Nasir Ud
Zhang, Li
Zhou, Yunhao
Chen, Ziliang
Yao, Yuhui
Yang, Zihan
Yang, Yatao
coredata.dc:description: Strong demand for electric vehicles and energy storage applications has led to a rapid expansion of the battery sector. Laser welding is widely used in lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing companies due to its high energy density and capability to join different materials. Welding quality plays a vital role in the durability and effectiveness of welding structures. Therefore, it is essential to monitor welding defects to ensure welds quality. Manual inspection, analysis and evaluation of welding defect images is difficult due to the non-uniformity in their shape, position, and size. Hence the use of deep learning techniques to identify welding defects is more accurate and reliable due to the adequate training data samples, which helps to identify welding defects with greater accuracy. In this study, we present a novel collection of 3,736 laser welding images which are labeled with eight classes. This dataset contains both normal and defective classes collected from a Dade Laser Chinese production line. Moreover, we introduce a modified loss function that integrates cross entropy and complement objective training. The EfficientNetB6 model is trained with the newly introduced loss function that performed efficiently and achieved a promising accuracy during the classification phase. The experimental results demonstrate the significance of the proposed loss function and the sufficiency of EfficientNetB6 model on the laser welding dataset. Compared to other models, EfficientNetB6 performs better.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laser welding
Data sampling
Complement objective training
Multi-classification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Shows the battery covering plate and electrodes. (b) Displays laser spot welding machine for power batteries.
A general overview of laser welding and testing equipment for acquiring dataset.
Flowchart of the entire laser welding operation.
CMOS digital camera with welding battery modules.

Eight types of battery welding defects with labels: (a) the left side shows the coveriswrong image while the right side shows the weldinghole image (b) the left side indicates the continuoushole image while the right side indicates the lackofweld image (c) the left side illustrates the burn image while the right side illustrates the shifting image (d) the left side describes the normal image while the right side describes the weldtoohigh image.
The smooth dataset distribution is presented in the form of training and testing data.

An illustration of proposed approach of laser welding dataset for multi-classification. The upper left pink color cylinder shows the laser welding dataset split up into two main branches (1) images and (2) labels with XML sheet. Next, we pre-process the dataset and downsize the original dataset into 512*512*1 pixels; up-sampling is used for unbalanced classes. Furthermore, we split the dataset into 80% training and 20% testing. EfficientNetB6 (ENU) used for training, and our study(OS) describes the proposed modified loss function were used in our approach for the Up-sample dataset; the orange block shows the trained model, the sky blue block indicates multi-classification, the last mix color block describes the four metrics.
(a) Represents confusion metric using proposed loss function (b) represents confusion metric without loss function.
Performance classifiers (a) represent the Macro-Precision-score, (b) represent the Macro-Recall-score, (c) represent the Macro-F1-score.

Receiver operator characteristic description (ROC) (a) represents roc curve utilizing the proposed loss function, (b) represents roc curve without the proposed loss function. In which class 0 represent coveriswrong, class 1 shows the weldinghole, class 2 indicates continuoushole, the class 3 describes the lackofweld, class 4 shows the burn class, class 5 illustrates the shifting class, and the last two, class 6 and 7 displays the normal and weldtoohigh classes respectively.
Partitioning the training and testing images for smooth, data sampling.
Demonstrates the pseudo-code for the classification methodology used in this study.

entry.bold: Algorithm 1: Pseudo-Code of Laser Welding Image Classification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different DL architecture using the smooth, up-sampling and proposed method.
Comparison result of data sampling and our approach.

head.ce:title: Laser welding defects detection in lithium-ion battery poles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electronics and Information Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electronics and Information Engineering, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electronics and Information Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strong demand for electric vehicles and energy storage applications has led to a rapid expansion of the battery sector. Laser welding is widely used in lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing companies due to its high energy density and capability to join different materials. Welding quality plays a vital role in the durability and effectiveness of welding structures. Therefore, it is essential to monitor welding defects to ensure welds quality. Manual inspection, analysis and evaluation of welding defect images is difficult due to the non-uniformity in their shape, position, and size. Hence the use of deep learning techniques to identify welding defects is more accurate and reliable due to the adequate training data samples, which helps to identify welding defects with greater accuracy. In this study, we present a novel collection of 3,736 laser welding images which are labeled with eight classes. This dataset contains both normal and defective classes collected from a Dade Laser Chinese production line. Moreover, we introduce a modified loss function that integrates cross entropy and complement objective training. The EfficientNetB6 model is trained with the newly introduced loss function that performed efficiently and achieved a promising accuracy during the classification phase. The experimental results demonstrate the significance of the proposed loss function and the sufficiency of EfficientNetB6 model on the laser welding dataset. Compared to other models, EfficientNetB6 performs better.

ce:section.ce:para: Laser welding, the most technically advanced form of welding, connects metals and thermoplastics together to create a weld through the use of a high-precision laser beam. The applications of laser welding span across a diverse array of industries, particularly in the automotive industry [1]. The increase and rapid development of electric vehicles is driving the demand for Lithium-ion Batteries (LIBs) [2,3]. LIBs are made of various electrochemical elementary cells composed of an anode, and a cathode, which are electrically separated by a separator film [4], in which electrodes are the most important issue among them [5]. A polar side (electrode) is typically attached to the terminal of the battery plate to prevent unpredictable power losses. The polar side is made of an aluminum alloy material, requiring a narrow weld joint and great welding precision. For this reason, laser welding is used in the manufacturing business because it is efficient, precise, and provides stunning welds [6].

Laser welding is a thermal conversion process; therefore, the parameters and workpieces must be extremely precise. Minor deviations in the welding process can result in serious defects, like collapse, cracks, porosity, burn, welding hole, etc, thus affecting the quality of the welding process [7,8]. In addition, welding quality is also affected by the types of welding materials used and the types of welded joints [9,10]. Laser welding operation is primarily monitored with four kinds of sensors in the welding process (1) acoustic [11,12], (2) visual [13,14], (3) optical [15,16] and (4) thermal [17,18] sensors. During the welding operation, sensors are used to measure the changes in characteristic quantities [19,20] and make a link between the dynamic trends of characteristic quantities and the resulted welding effect. Due to the large number of image data required for real-time visual testing, it is difficult to implement it on the production line. Furthermore, the intense light used in laser welding can cause image saturation, resulting in a lower level of precision rate. Additionally, because of the inevitable background noise in assembly line, it is not appropriate to embed acoustic sensors into the laser welding operation supervision system to identify defects. Due to the complexity and varying nature of manufacturing and production methods, different instruments and methods are needed for laser welding defect detection. In most cases, one device or procedure will not provide adequate detection accuracy, although it may seem feasible to integrate all of the corresponding instruments and methods into one system. However, it is inconceivable because of the high workload and complexity of detecting every possible issue in every production process [21].

The emergence and rapid evolution of machine learning [22] and deep learning [23,24] based methods has made great strides in recent years, in which intelligent fault detection is a research priority and a key area of development. Artificial neural networks (ANNs) is most commonly used for intelligent fault diagnosis: detection, recognition, and classification [25,26], that evolved from a single approach of classification to a fusion of multiple approaches [27,28]. To investigate battery faults detection and improve safety measures, some preliminary researchers work on welding defects. Xie et al. [29] developed an improved adaptive boosting (AdaBoost) and decision tree-based inspection approach for solder junctions. Although this approach obtained good accuracy, it relies on manual knowledge to extract the relevant features, thus reducing the execution efficiency. Zhu et al. [30] presented a multiscale attention semantic segmentation algorithm, which is highly accurate and efficient for the safety vent dataset, and this architecture segmented defects of different sizes and shapes in real time and achieved more refined segmentation results. Yang et al. [31] introduced a lightweight deep-learning model for the inspection of a laser welding defect. However, the features used by the model were randomly distributed, which was challenging to determine the precise defects. Vasilev et al. [32] using non-contact air-coupled ultrasound, which was able to analyze weld defects instantly. Yang et al. [33] used a Visual Geometry Group (VGG) to classify laser welding defects and inspect laser welding quality. The lighting environment played a significant role on the quality of the photographs. The majority of the deep learning-based approaches for laser welding defects are unable to distinguish between varieties of categories of information fully. For example, in our previous work [34], we applied a machine learning-based approach but did not achieve the highest classification accuracy.

This study presents a novel laser welding dataset that contains 3,736 labeled images of electrodes. The images were taken using a complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) digital camera. The dataset was constructed in partnership with Dade Laser Chinese production line. We also construct laser welding defect detection model based on this dataset. A modified loss function approach is introduced to handle the accuracy of defect classes and improve the accuracy ratio in classification. In particular, the classification issue is addressed by developing a pre-trained model in the setting of deep learning. The current proposed approach provides a useful solution not only for handling model loss but also for solving a classification problem by maximizing the ground truth class probability while simultaneously minimizing the likelihoods of complement classes (non–ground truth classes). The main contributions of this paper are:

ce:list-item.ce:para: A novel laser welding defect dataset is developed that includes seven defect classes and one normal class. The up-sampling technique is used to overcome the imbalanceness in the dataset according to the majority class labels.
A modified loss function is introduced and nine deep-learning models are used for the purpose of classification/detection on the developed dataset.

The performance of nine deep learning models is evaluated and compared on four metrics. The obtained results showed that the EfficientNetB6 model achieved the highest accuracy of 94.26% and performed better than other models.

ce:section.ce:para: The rest of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 provides the details for the data acquisition and laser welding defect dataset development. Section 3 describes the dataset description and annotation. Section 4 describes the methodology for the classification/detection of laser welding defects. Section 5 provides the experimental results and discussion for the evaluation of the methodology. Finally, Section 6 concludes the paper with some remarks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An overview of dataset acquisition process
ce:section.ce:para: Our dataset acquisition process includes two types of machines; a laser welding machine is used for the welding process, whereas a testing machine is used for the testing purpose of the experiment. In our scenario laser spot welding machine (LSW) is used. This machine has the ability to weld a significant number of electrodes on the plate of batteries in a very short period of time, as shown in Fig. 1.

A main waterline was used to transport batteries from one place to another. It is composed of three branches that are
11050
m m
in length,
2550
m m
in width, and
2050
m m
in height (L × D × H). In order to accomplish laser welding, a laser welding machine and testing equipment are installed accordingly to meet the laser welding criteria for battery shells of new energy vehicles as well as an online marking machine, an automatic assembly line, and other units. Among them, the laser welding machine is the first to work, mainly to complete the welding work of the battery. A testing device examines the weld seam for short circuits or seals, whereas a label printing device prints the label. The laser welding machines at the front end of an assembly line are also used to eliminate substandard items from the production line.
(1) The laser welding equipment is equipped with both loading and unloading devices; laser welding equipment can reduce welding deformations and boost the efficiency of manufacturing processes.

(2) During the welding process, each of the two welding tables is equipped with an electric manipulator, and the welding area, as well as the feeding area, are separated to ensure the safety of the operator during the working process. The experimental setup for this experiment is shown in Fig. 2.

When the battery case is assembled and ready, it is placed on the conveyor belt and then passed through the conveyor belt to the welding area. The material is loaded by the welding manipulator, then assembled, and fixed by the start-up jig. Position clamping, using CMOS to detect the seam before welding to ensure the welding seam spacing is consistent and suitable before welding. The CMOS camera positioning error within
0 . 05
m m
. After the battery is packaged and welded by a laser welding machine, the welding penetration depth can reach
0 . 3
m m ∼ 0 . 6
m m
, and the melting width can reach
0 . 8
m m ∼ 1 . 3
m m
. The overall look of the welding seam is tidy after laser welding. The welding seam is exquisitely formed, and the welding deformation is less than 0.5 millimeters. At the same time, a smoke exhaust and dust removal device are equipped above the welding head to remove the smoke and dust generated during the welding process in time to maintain the production environment. The welding apparatus utilizes pneumatic control. This is capable of automatically positioning and clamping the process, which can use the conveying shaft to correctly send the battery shell to the spot where it will be welded. CMOS is utilized throughout the process of welding in order to monitor the welding in real-time. The fixture may automatically change the size to accommodate the size of the battery shell that needs to be welded. Fig. 3 illustrates the entire laser welding process.

A CMOS digital camera (BASLER Basler acA2500-14um camera with UTRON HS2514J lens) and a white annular LED light source (OPT-RI7030) with a light intensity range of 0–255 levels were used to capture the welding area images for the laser welding AOI system shown in Fig. 4. In spite of the fact that the majority of recent AOI systems employ a CCD camera, which is more costly than the CMOS camera [35]. Modern CMOS cameras have good image quality and are frequently used for industrial inspection [36]. In this research, a CMOS digital camera with 5 megapixels was used, and it was able to reliably capture a high resolution of the welding area. Therefore, a two-dimensional (2D) gray image might accurately depict the 3D shape information of the welding area [37]. The welded component in the image was made more visible by applying a white annular light source to the object at a roughly 90-degree angle. The white LED light beams were applied to the battery surface during operation, and then they were reflected into the camera. We were able to create these case samples throughout the image acquisition process because deep learning needs diverse samples to better imitate in real application settings. We employed many different shooting distances, varying roughly from 45 cm to 55 cm, together images four times in all. In addition, we randomized the brightness of the light sources, which ranged from approximately 55 to 150 on the brightness scale. With these techniques, the images that were consistently obtained each time had varying brightness levels and welding area sizes; as a result, they met the prerequisites of the deep learning algorithm. By using a white annular source of light in place of three LED lights of contrasting colors (red, green, and blue), [38]; therefore, it is suitable to be used in an industrial setting.

The dataset associated with this paper includes 3,736 grayscale images divided into eight different classes. The original dataset was captured in the dimensions of 2448*2048 pixels from Dade Laser Chinese production line, which is later used this dataset for training and testing, as well as for identification models resource. This study examines deep-learning classification problem using various models and inputs image sizes. Increasing image resolution for training with deep learning models frequently requires additional computer resources and a larger maximum batch size and is quite huge for training with deep learning approaches. Additionally, memory restrictions are brought about by big input image sizes. Due to the high computational complexity and requirements that follow, deep learning models take a long time to train and infer [39]. For instance, it can require a few days of continuous model training on computer hardware with Graphical Processing Units (GPU) for input images with a resolution of 2448*2048 pixels, which has the potential to be perceived as being inapplicable. Therefore, we down-scaled the image size into a specific size according to model training requirements and the time budget. The pixels of the downsized image are in the JPG file format. Once the dataset samples are completed, our laboratory group painstakingly analyzed each sample over the course of three weeks to determine the various defects and normal images, which were then annotated with a specific name according to the defect in the images and created the XML file sheet with every image defect. Throughout the manufacturing process, few flaws were detected due to the difficulty of spotting them. Consequently, we sought the assistance of specialists from related fields in order to locate the specific problem.

Based on the results of the above experiments, we were able to identify eight distinct classes of laser welding images, each of which was assigned a specific name based on the data samples: (1) coveriswrong, (2) weldinghole, (3) continuoushole, (4) lackofweld, (5) burn, (6) shifting, (7) normal, and (8) weldtoohigh. It appears that the majority of these classes consist of images with flaws. The term ‘coveriswrong’ corresponds to an error that occur when an inappropriate cover is chosen. Despite the fact that LSW is capable to weld a large number of battery poles on a battery plate quickly, its products are often defective due to complex parameter configurations. In the situation when the laser pulse energy is high, it can lead to ‘weldingholes’, ‘continuousholes’, and ‘burn’ defects. ‘Weldingholes’ are holes that are created as a result of inadequate adjustment of the welding parameters. Conversely ‘continuousholes’ refer to holes that are created continuously or successively. A related defect, ‘lackofweld’ arises when the heating duration is too short. In addition, ‘burn’ defects are provoked by excessive laser power and input heating power. Furthermore, when the pole was not completely inserted into the design acupoint where it was placed, a large white gap can appear between the pole and the cover plate. This can led to a defects termed ‘shifting’. The welding products have a smooth welding surface without welding slag, pores, or other contaminants, and are firmly attached to the cover plate with excellent air tightness termed as ‘normal’. On the other hand, ‘weldtoohigh’ refers to a term used to describe a condition where the weld bead is excessively high. The images with the precise welding name provided by the expert is shown in Fig. 5.

Since no dataset is publicly available that has laser welding defects and undefected labels, a laser welding dataset constructed from Chinese production lines that was used to test our prediction method in real-world scenarios. For the sake of our assignment of classifying the laser welding dataset, we define eight different class labels. Next, we mark each image to determine which contains defects and which does not, then separate them into training and testing sets. In terms of formal definition and image classification, welding sets are technically classified as follows:

Given the training data
D

=

represents the labels, in which n describes the training samples and 1 represents the number of feature in each sample. The task is to learn a feature extractor
g

f

)
. In addition, based on the knowledge of prior testing data, we define eight classification jobs.

There were a total of 3,736 samples collected from the automatic manufacturing line, of which 466 were normal samples, and 3,270 were defective samples. A random distribution was performed using the Scikit-learn function to divide the dataset for eight-classification into training and testing sets. There were 2,988 samples accounted for in the training set, which is 80% of the entire dataset, and 748 in the testing set. After training the model with the training set, the model’s dependability can be evaluated using the testing dataset. Fig. 6 illustrates the classification information for both the training and testing sets of a multi-classification dataset.

Similarly, we divided the testing dataset into eight classes for multi-class classification, which accounted for 20% of the entire dataset. Each of the automated LSW machines on the manufacturing line includes an integrated AOI system from which all of the images are obtained. All the images underwent a two-step preparation process in which the images were first cropped to a specified size of 512*512 pixels from the original dataset 2448*2048 and then normalized. Although the classes were imbalanced, that may cause models over fitting toward majority class data. Therefore, to resolve the class imbalance problem, up-sampling is a well-known and effective technique used to balance the dataset. With an input size of 512*512, it may be possible to trade off between the model’s training accuracy and inference time, as smaller input sizes may completely hide potential micro-weld flaws during training and lead to subpar accuracy. In the same manner, if the input size is too large, a model’s parameters will rise, which will also lengthen the amount of time needed for training and inference. The development of ground truth labels for supervised learning, or correctly classifying the category of the initial samples, is the most crucial aspect of producing a dataset. We performed this step by employing human annotators who meticulously examined the source images under a microscope to eliminate the possibility of mislabeling caused by microscopic faults concealed in welds. Table 1 represents the training and testing images.

This section formulates the problem and the underlying assumptions, followed by a description of the method of the loss function and the specific strategies utilized to solve the classification problem and improve the model performance [40].

In this work, we develop a newly modified function that utilizes deep learning techniques to analyze laser-welded images. Our work greatly improves the robustness of this method for data sampling, and image classification; both cross entropy and complement objective training are used simultaneously during the training phase, as shown in Fig. 7.

For this purpose, we employed a variety of neural network designs with the Tensor Flow framework to achieve our goal. Cross entropy is becoming more prevalent due to the widespread use of deep neural networks as their primary training objective [41]. Essentially, minimizing cross entropy refers to maximizing the likelihood of disjoint classes as the primary training objective. Cross-entropy has emerged as the de facto standard for many different training goals, including classification and sequence generation [42].

Suppose
y ∈
{ 0 , 1 }
K
is the label of the

sample in the one-hot encoded format and

is the predicted probabilities, and the term cross entropy is the measure of the difference between the true label and the predicted probabilities,
H

is a neural network and x is a sample. Although training with the cross entropy as the primary target has yielded remarkable results, one restriction has been brought to our attention, and that is the fact that in most cases, it uses ground-truth information as demonstrated in Eq. (1); the information included within complement classes (i.e., wrong classes) has been mostly disregarded, due to the dot product calculation with the one-hot encoded
y
i
, all the predicted probability apart from
y
ˆ
i g
are nullified. Thus the model behavior is not explicitly optimized for classes other than the ground truth, but it is indirectly minimized when
y
ˆ
i g
is maximized since the probability distributions of the predicted probabilities add up to one. Neutralizing the predicted probabilities of complement classes is one technique to exploit the information provided by the complement classes. Conventionally, training with cross entropy as the primary objective tries to maximize the predicted probability of the ground-truth class in Eq. (2). However, the existing complement objective training [43] also optimizes the complement objective in order to neutralize the predicted probabilities of the complement classes. To accomplish this, we introduce a modified loss function that integrates cross-entropy and complement objective training in our scenario, a new training methodology that fulfills this optimization goal without affecting the model’s primary goal. Training with this objective function identifies the parameters that suppress the probability of complement classes while boosting the probability of ground truth class simultaneously without losing the primary aim hence increasing the model’s confidence in the ground-truth class.
(2)

, C(.) is the complimentary entropy and N , K the overall samples size and the overall class size. Table 2 describes the proposed methodology study of classification.

The performance of classifiers used in this work is evaluated with five metrics:(1) Accuracy, (2) Macro-Precision,(3) Macro-Recall,(4) Macro-F1, and (5) ROC-AUC. We used the following metrics in our study to assess multi-class classifiers. In which four key and crucial outcomes are (true negative (TN), true positive (TP), false positive (FP), and false negative (FN)) are typically used to assess the effectiveness of classification analysis. These four results served as the basis for the performance criteria of the deep learning models techniques and their effectiveness assessment. This classification is based on multi-classification and the total number of samples size in the dataset is indicated by N (class= N ).

j = 1
p
2 ×
P r e c i s i o
n
j
× R e c a l
l
j
P r e c i s i o
n
j
+ R e c a l
l
j

To evaluate the dataset, we divided the total dataset into training images and testing images with a resolution of 512*512*1 pixels for smooth data and for data sampling. We compiled several deep learning models [44] such as MobileNetV2, DenseNet121_net, Vgg-16, Image classification with EANet, Swin transformer, Vision Transformer, CNN, ResNet50V2, and EfficientNetB6; the experimental results of all networks are summarized in Table 3, we use a server with high-performance NVIDIA GTX 2080Ti 11 GB GPU, an [Dual Channel] Intel Xeon (R) Silver 4110 CPU @ 2.10 GHz 16 CPU with 256 G RAM running Linux Ubuntu 20.04 LTS with CUDA 11.7.0 and CUDDN version 8.5 libraries for all networks. Since we trained a few networks from scratch using the available dataset, while a few used transfer learning to save training time. We leveraged the pre-trained architecture provided by the Keras application and the GitHub model repository [45].

All networks are trained for 500 epochs using Adam optimizer, softmax function, learning rate 1e-3, batch size 32, and categorical cross-entropy loss simultaneously. EfficientNetB6 performed the best model of all the architecture and achieved promising accuracy and Macro-Precision by training with the proposed training loss function. This statistic demonstrates that based on the loss function and up-sampling data during the testing phase, we have nearly achieved 94.265% accuracy. In contrast, the maximum accuracy for the smooth and up-sampling dataset is 92.7% and 85.5%, respectively. It makes evident that there is a significant variance between loss function,up-sampling, and smooth data.

To evaluate the performance of an individual classification method, the confusion metric was used to analyze the results of OS and ENU approach, as shown in Fig. 8(a,b). There are eight distinct classes in this method. In order to gain a clearer picture of the relationship between the true labels situated on the y -axis and the predicted labels on the x -axis, we opted to represent this confusion matrix as a heat map. These eight classes are expressed in the heat map according to the names of the labels leveraged in our study. In which Fig. 8(a) indicates the confusion metric of EfficientnetB6 utilizing the modified loss function. It shows that the coveriswrong, the burn, and the shifting classes had the highest prediction accuracy of 100%. While the predicted accuracy for weldinghole, continuoushole, lackofweld, normal, weldtoohigh was 83%, 81%, 98%,97% 93% accuracy, respectively. A comparison metric in Fig. 8(b) against confusion metrics with modified loss function indicates that the coveriswrong is correctly classified and achieved 100% accuracy, whereas weldinghole, continuoushole, lackofweld, burn, shifting, normal, and weldtoohigh had the prediction accuracy of 87%, 75%, 98%, 98%,97%, 96%, 88% respectively. Thus, with the use of the modified loss function, our method achieved the highest accuracy overall. The below-mentioned metrics result were achieved based on the up-sampling dataset.

A series of Macro-Precision, Macro-Recall, and Macro-F1-score graphs for eight defects of laser welding flaws is presented in Fig. 9 (a,b,c). Based on the Macro-precision, Macro-recall, and Macro-F1-score, it can be concluded that our approach achieved high performance in evaluation metrics. This study reports accuracy and F1-score to describe the classification performance of a deep learning model; however, the F1-Score is taken into consideration for the final evaluation.

In order to ensure a comprehensive comparison of OS and ENU, we examined multiple types of metrics, precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy. We determined that OS performed well and achieved promising accuracy in laser welding fault classification. Our analysis also focused on the computational complexity of both models. Both ENU and OS models were also assessed for computational complexity by measuring how long it took to train and test each model. A negligible difference in computational time is caused by the loss function computation of the OS model compared to the ENU model. Table 4 summarizes the result of the data sample, our approach, and computational time in hours.

In deep learning, graphical representation plays an essential role in evaluating the performance of multiclass classification problems. The ROC curves are plotted to show the performance of multiclass classifiers. Additionally, their Area Under Curve (AUC) can also be used to access the classification ability and effectiveness of their techniques in a particular setting. The AUC on a ROC curve is equal to the probability, which provides a measure of how well a model is separable. AUCs around 1 indicate a highly separable model, while AUCs below zero indicate that the model is poor in performance. Fig. 10 (a,b) shows the ROC-AUC score, illustrating how successfully each class is classified by the model using all potential thresholds or how fixable each class is.

In this study, we provide a novel laser welding dataset, which consists of eight kinds of images. This dataset is used for training and testing on various deep-learning networks. In which, a simple and effective way to classify multi-class images was demonstrated by the experimental results on the EfficientNetB6 model. The EfficientNetB6 model has been trained with a new modified loss function and fine-tuned accordingly to the deep learning algorithm and saved its weights. The model has been modified to fit the new laser welding dataset, and the model accuracy has been increased after a few training samples. It is found that EffiecientNetB6 with modified loss function had a higher accuracy value than other deep learning-based algorithms. The accuracy of our test data set is outperformed, and we achieved a significant result of 94.26%, which is surprisingly good considering that the dataset is quite straightforward, but for the rest of the other 5.74%, it is more challenging to classify. For future research, we will collect a large number of images because our dataset is unbalanced, which may cause model over-fitting and affect the accuracy of the deep learning models. Secondly, we will use some ablation and modification experiments for end-to-end solutions in the model to achieve the rest of the challenging flaws in laser welding image classification.

Nasir Ud Din: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Li Zhang: Writting– review & editing. Yunhao Zhou: Visualization & data curation. Ziliang Chen: Formal analysis. Yuhui Yao: Investigation. Zihan Yang: Formal analysis, Supervision. Yatao Yang: Project administration, Resources Funding.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Shenzhen-Hong Kong Joint Innovation Foundation, PR China under Grant (SGDX20190919094401725) and was partially supported by the Shenzhen Science and Technology Program, PR China (JCYJ20210324093806017).
